verse.arabic_text: كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَـٰرَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ
verse.translation: [This is] a blessed Book which We have revealed to you, that they might reflect upon its verses and that those of understanding would be reminded.

theme_question.correct: The primary purpose of the Quran's revelation is for deep reflection and remembrance.
options: The primary purpose of the Quran's revelation is for deep reflection and remembrance.
The Quran's main function is to be recited for blessings without understanding.
The Quran was revealed only as a historical record of past nations.
The Book was sent down exclusively for the people of Makkah.

educational_notes.verse_context: This verse is a powerful declaration about the very nature and purpose of the Quran. It comes within Surah Sad, which discusses themes of prophethood, patience, and accountability. After recounting stories of past prophets like David and Solomon to console Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ), Allah emphasizes that this revealed Book is not mere storytelling but a blessed source of guidance meant for profound contemplation.
educational_notes.theme_explanation: The theme is "Tadabbur al-Quran"—reflecting upon the Quran. The verse highlights that the Quran's blessings are unlocked not just through ritual recitation, but through deep, thoughtful engagement with its meanings. It invites "Ulul-Albab" (people of understanding) to ponder its signs, derive lessons, and remember Allah. This active reflection is what transforms the reader and brings the guidance of the scripture to life.
educational_notes.surah_overview: Surah Sad (ص) is a Makkan surah named after the Arabic letter with which it begins. It addresses the disbelief of the Quraysh, comforting the Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ) by narrating the trials and triumphs of previous prophets, particularly Dawud (David) and Sulayman (Solomon), and the patience of Ayyub (Job). The surah powerfully contrasts the fate of the righteous in Paradise with the despair of the disbelievers in Hell, and concludes by reaffirming the core message of Tawhid (the Oneness of Allah).
